title: Bill Cosby Ordered to Pay Nearly $60M for 1972 Sexual Assault
description: A California jury has found Bill Cosby liable for the 1972 drugging and sexual assault of Donna Motsinger, awarding her $59.25 million in damages.
lede: A Santa Monica jury has ordered Bill Cosby to pay $59.25 million to a woman he drugged and sexually assaulted more than fifty years ago. The verdict caps a nearly two-week trial over allegations that the 88-year-old actor incapacitated the victim with pills and wine following a 1972 comedy show.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: Following a nearly two-week trial in Santa Monica, California, a jury found 88-year-old Bill Cosby liable for the sexual battery and assault of Donna Motsinger. The jury awarded Motsinger a total of $59.25 million, which includes $17.5 million for past damages, $1.75 million for future damages, and $40 million in punitive damages.

Motsinger filed the lawsuit in 2023, alleging that in 1972, Cosby invited her to his stand-up comedy performance in San Carlos, California. At the time, she was working as a server at a restaurant in nearby Sausalito, and both individuals were in their 30s.

According to the lawsuit, Cosby provided Motsinger with wine and two pills he claimed were aspirin. She testified to drifting in and out of consciousness as two men placed her into a limousine, later waking up naked in her own home with the realization she had been drugged and raped.

sections.heading: The Defense's Stance
paragraphs: During the trial, Cosby did not take the stand to testify. His legal team argued that the allegations were fundamentally flawed, asserting they were based almost entirely on speculation and assumption.

Cosby's lawyers emphasized that Motsinger herself admitted she had no clear memory of the specific events that transpired after losing consciousness. Following the verdict, Cosby's lawyer, Jennifer Bonjean, expressed disappointment and confirmed their intention to appeal the decision.
The actor has consistently denied all allegations of sexual misconduct, maintaining his innocence despite accusations from numerous women over the decades.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: This verdict is the latest legal reckoning for the once-revered "America's Dad," who was previously convicted of sexual assault in a Pennsylvania court in 2018. That conviction, involving Temple University sports administrator Andrea Constand—who also testified in Motsinger's trial—was later overturned by the state's Supreme Court, leading to Cosby's release after serving nearly three years in prison.

Cosby was the first major celebrity to face criminal conviction during the #MeToo movement, a cultural shift that gained momentum in late 2017. The allegations brought by Motsinger echo the claims of at least 60 other women who have accused the comedian of similar patterns of rape, sexual assault, and harassment.

While Cosby has settled multiple civil lawsuits in the past and has been mandated to pay damages in other cases, Monday's $59.25 million award stands as likely the largest financial penalty he has faced in a single case.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: Cosby's legal team is preparing to appeal the California jury's verdict, initiating what could be a lengthy appellate process to contest the $59.25 million judgment. The defense will likely challenge the admissibility of certain testimonies and the statute of limitations applications.

For Motsinger, the verdict represents a long-awaited resolution. She stated that the lawsuit was never primarily about the money, but rather about holding Cosby accountable for actions she has carried the weight of for over fifty years.

The ruling may also influence pending or future civil litigation against Cosby, as other survivors and legal experts observe how the courts handle decades-old allegations under newly enacted lookback window legislation in various states.
